semantics: create owns one hidden precise-heap owner, one private VM reference, and one exposed precise sibling realm; allocation failure releases all three without publishing a partial lifecycle.
mini mode selects the small heap tag and ordinary mode selects the large heap tag while both preserve one VM identity for their exposed realm.
console, execution-context, eval-mode, worker, and heap-mode metadata are copied into the lifecycle record without dereferencing opaque embedder pointers.
positive worker or macro execution-context identifiers above one are installed exactly and remain process-unique; main/default globals receive a non-zero lazy identity.

test isolation creates a fresh realm with an independent non-cell arena on the same precise heap, VM, JIT owner, and GlobalSymbolRegistry, with new globals, intrinsics, module state, jobs, and object graph.
test isolation transfers the inherited process lookup to the replacement and assigns the retired realm a fresh unpublished identifier.
the retired realm loses the private creator reference; an explicit public retain preserves its callable Context only until the matching release and never republishes the inherited process identity.

VM-owned private EncodedJSValue boxes live in the hidden owner arena; an explicit protect keeps an outgoing realm cell valid after Context retirement, and matching unprotect triggers full collection plus physical realm release.
precise collections rebuild the weak canonical private-object box map, dropping dead cells and rekeying protected cells after relocation without changing accepted ABI signatures.

replacement retires old global, module, job, handle, timer, waiter, and embedding roots; external class, buffer, and string owners finalize exactly once before the independent realm arena is reclaimed.
destruct-on-exit atomically consumes the private lifecycle once, requests terminal shutdown, releases the private VM owner, then releases the creator context reference.
null, foreign, stale, and repeated lifecycle operations are inert; wrong-thread isolation returns null and wrong-thread teardown leaves ownership untouched.
ordinary public contexts are never consumed by the private teardown boundary.
the process execution-context registry is retired before the final VM allocation is freed, so concurrent identifier lookup cannot publish a stale global.
Home and Bun compile and link independent executables against their respective private ABI tag profiles.
